1. Press [MODE] and ensure DEGREE is selected
2. Enter the expression

desmos.instructions: Type the expression (ensure degree mode via wrench icon)

calculator.description: Calculate the length of side AB

steps.title: Visualize the decomposition
steps.content:
To use the **Right-Angled Decomposition** approach, we start by dropping a perpendicular altitude from vertex $A$ down to the side $BC$. Let's call the point where it hits the base $D$.

This splits our original triangle into two right-angled triangles:
- $\triangle ABD$ on the left (containing $\angle B = 60^{\circ}$)
- $\triangle ADC$ on the right (containing $\angle C = 40^{\circ}$)

Let $h$ be the height of the altitude $AD$.

steps.title: Express base segments
steps.content:
The altitude divides the base $BC$ into two segments, $BD$ and $DC$. We know the total length is $10 \text{ cm}$.

Using the tangent ratio ($\tan \theta = \frac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}}$) in each right-angled triangle, we can express these segments in terms of the height $h$:

In $\triangle ABD$:
$$ \tan 60^{\circ} = \frac{h}{BD} \implies BD = \frac{h}{\tan 60^{\circ}} $$

In $\triangle ADC$:
$$ \tan 40^{\circ} = \frac{h}{DC} \implies DC = \frac{h}{\tan 40^{\circ}} $$

steps.title: Solve for height
steps.content:
Since the two base segments must add up to the total length of side $BC$, we can set up an equation:

$$ BD + DC = 10 $$

Substitute the expressions we found in Step 1:

$$ \frac{h}{\tan 60^{\circ}} + \frac{h}{\tan 40^{\circ}} = 10 $$

Factor out $h$ to solve for it:

$$ h \left( \frac{1}{\tan 60^{\circ}} + \frac{1}{\tan 40^{\circ}} \right) = 10 $$

$$ h = \frac{10}{\frac{1}{\tan 60^{\circ}} + \frac{1}{\tan 40^{\circ}}} $$

steps.title: Calculate side AB
steps.content:
Now that we have an expression for the height $h$, we can find the length of side $AB$. In the right-angled triangle $\triangle ABD$, side $AB$ is the hypotenuse.

Using the sine ratio ($\sin \theta = \frac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}}$):

$$ \sin 60^{\circ} = \frac{h}{AB} $$

Rearranging for $AB$:

$$ AB = \frac{h}{\sin 60^{\circ}} $$

Substituting our value for $h$:

$$ AB = \frac{10}{\sin 60^{\circ} \left( \frac{1}{\tan 60^{\circ}} + \frac{1}{\tan 40^{\circ}} \right)} \approx 6.53 \text{ cm} $$

ti84.instructions: Use the previous answer (Ans) for h: Ans / sin(60)

desmos.instructions: Take the previous result and divide by sin(60 deg)

calculator.description: Calculate the final length of AB

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The length of side $AB$ is approximately **6.53 cm**.

*Note: This method confirms the result you would get using the Sine Rule, which relies on finding the third angle $\angle A = 80^{\circ}$ first.*

parts.content: Find the area of triangle $ABC$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to use area formula: Area $= \frac{1}{2}(AB)(BC) \sin B$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute correct values: Area $= \frac{1}{2}(6.53)(10) \sin 60^{\circ}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Area $= 28.3 \text{ cm}^{2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the strategy
steps.content:
To find the area of the triangle, we can use the formula that requires two sides and the included angle. We are given side $BC = 10$ and $\angle B = 60^\circ$. If we find the length of side $AB$, we can use these two sides and the included angle $B$ to calculate the area.

We will determine side $AB$ using the **Sine Rule**, which first requires us to know all three angles.

steps.title: Find the third angle
steps.content:
The sum of angles in a triangle is always $180^\circ$. We can calculate $\angle A$ using the given angles $\angle B = 60^\circ$ and $\angle C = 40^\circ$:

$$\begin{aligned} \angle A &= 180^\circ - (60^\circ + 40^\circ) \\ \angle A &= 80^\circ \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate the third angle

steps.title: Set up the Sine Rule
steps.content:
Now we use the Sine Rule to find side $c$ (which is length $AB$). We use the pair ($a, \angle A$) and ($c, \angle C$).

Using the formula from the data booklet:
$$\frac{a}{\sin A} = \frac{c}{\sin C}$$

Substitute the known values ($a = BC = 10$):
$$\frac{10}{\sin 80^\circ} = \frac{AB}{\sin 40^\circ}$$

steps.title: Calculate side AB
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to solve for $AB$:

$$AB = \frac{10 \sin 40^\circ}{\sin 80^\circ}$$

$$AB \approx 6.527\dots \text{ cm}$$

gdc.instructions: Ensure mode is set to DEGREES

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is set to DEGREES

desmos.instructions: Ensure mode is set to DEGREES

steps.title: Apply Area Formula
steps.content:
Now we calculate the area using sides $AB$ and $BC$ and the included angle $B$. Note that the data booklet formula uses variables $a, b, C$, but the concept is always $\frac{1}{2} \times \text{side}_1 \times \text{side}_2 \times \sin(\text{included angle})$.

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}(AB)(BC) \sin B$$

Substitute the values:
$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}(6.527\dots)(10) \sin 60^\circ$$

steps.title: Calculate final area
steps.content:
Perform the final calculation:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Area} &\approx 28.26\dots \\ \text{Area} &= 28.3 \text{ cm}^2 \text{ (3 s.f.)} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the previous answer for precision

ti84.instructions: Use the previous answer (Ans) for precision

desmos.instructions: Use the calculated value for AB

steps.title: Construct the altitude
steps.content:
To find the area using the altitude method, we first draw a perpendicular line (altitude) from vertex $A$ down to the base $BC$. Let's call the height of this altitude $h$.

This altitude splits the base $BC$ into two segments. Let's call the segment closest to angle $B$ "$x$" and the segment closest to angle $C$ "$y$".

Since the total length of $BC$ is given as $10 \text{ cm}$, we know that:
$$x + y = 10$$

steps.title: Express bases using tangent
steps.content:
Now we have two right-angled triangles. We can use the tangent ratio ($\tan \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}$) to express the base segments $x$ and $y$ in terms of the height $h$.

For the triangle with $\angle B = 60^{\circ}$:
$$\tan 60^{\circ} = \frac{h}{x} \implies x = \frac{h}{\tan 60^{\circ}}$$

For the triangle with $\angle C = 40^{\circ}$:
$$\tan 40^{\circ} = \frac{h}{y} \implies y = \frac{h}{\tan 40^{\circ}}$$

steps.title: Set up equation for height
steps.content:
Since the two base segments must add up to the total length of $BC$, we can set up an equation for $h$:

$$x + y = 10$$

Substituting our expressions from the previous step:

$$\frac{h}{\tan 60^{\circ}} + \frac{h}{\tan 40^{\circ}} = 10$$

steps.title: Solve for height
steps.content:
Factor out $h$ to solve the equation:

$$h \left( \frac{1}{\tan 60^{\circ}} + \frac{1}{\tan 40^{\circ}} \right) = 10$$

$$h = \frac{10}{\frac{1}{\tan 60^{\circ}} + \frac{1}{\tan 40^{\circ}}}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression to solve for h:

steps.title: Calculate height value
steps.content:
Using the calculator, we find the value of $h$:

$$h \approx \frac{10}{0.5774 + 1.1918} \approx \frac{10}{1.7692} \approx 5.653 \text{ cm}$$

steps.title: Calculate the area
steps.content:
Finally, use the basic area formula for a triangle, Area $= \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$:

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 5.653$$

$$\text{Area} \approx 28.3 \text{ cm}^2$$

This matches the markscheme answer.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Finding the range of a side length in a triangle given a condition on cos(θ) typically uses the cosine rule to relate cos of an angle to the three sides and then convert the inequality into bounds on side a.

parts.content: Find the range of possible values of $a$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to use the cosine formula: $\mathrm{AB}^{2}=\mathrm{AC}^{2}+\mathrm{BC}^{2}-2(\mathrm{AC})(\mathrm{BC}) \cos (\mathrm{ACB})$
- Substitute correct values: $4^{2}=a^{2}+3^{2}-2(3)(a) \cos \theta$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Rearrange to isolate $\cos \theta$: $16=a^{2}+9-6 a \cos \theta \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{a^{2}-7}{6 a}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Set up inequality using $\cos \theta > 0.25$: $\frac{a^{2}-7}{6 a}>\frac{1}{4}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Simplify to form quadratic inequality: $2 a^{2}-14>3 a \Rightarrow 2 a^{2}-3 a-14>0$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Solve the inequality for $a$: $a>\frac{7}{2}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Apply triangle inequality property: $\mathrm{BC}<\mathrm{AB}+\mathrm{AC}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute side lengths: $a<4+3 \Rightarrow a<7$ <marks>A1</marks>
- State the range: $a \in\left(\frac{7}{2}, 7\right)$ <marks>A1</marks>

8 marks total

steps.title: Identify sides and formula
steps.content: We are working with triangle $ABC$ where side $AC = 3$, side $AB = 4$, side $BC = a$, and the angle at $C$ is $\theta$. To relate the three sides and the angle $\theta$, we use the cosine rule from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet.

steps.title: Substitute values into cosine rule
steps.content:
In our triangle, the angle $\theta$ is at vertex $C$, so the opposite side is $c = AB = 4$. The adjacent sides are $b = AC = 3$ and $a = BC = a$. Substituting these into the formula:

$$4^2 = a^2 + 3^2 - 2(a)(3) \cos \theta$$

$$16 = a^2 + 9 - 6a \cos \theta$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate 4^2 and 3^2

steps.title: Isolate cos θ
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to make $\cos \theta$ the subject:

$$\begin{aligned} 16 &= a^2 + 9 - 6a \cos \theta \\ 6a \cos \theta &= a^2 + 9 - 16 \\ 6a \cos \theta &= a^2 - 7 \\ \cos \theta &= \frac{a^2 - 7}{6a} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set up the inequality
steps.content:
The question gives the condition $\cos \theta > 0.25$. Substitute our expression for $\cos \theta$:

$$\frac{a^2 - 7}{6a} > 0.25$$

Since $a$ is a side length, $a > 0$, so we can multiply both sides by $6a$ without flipping the inequality sign:

$$\begin{aligned} a^2 - 7 &> 0.25(6a) \\ a^2 - 7 &> 1.5a \\ 2a^2 - 14 &> 3a \quad \text{(multiply by 2 to clear decimals)} \\ 2a^2 - 3a - 14 &> 0 \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply 0.25 by 6

steps.title: Solve the quadratic inequality
steps.content:
To solve $2a^2 - 3a - 14 > 0$, we first find the roots of the corresponding equation $2a^2 - 3a - 14 = 0$. We can factor this quadratic:

$$(2a - 7)(a + 2) = 0$$

The roots are $a = \frac{7}{2} = 3.5$ and $a = -2$.

Since the quadratic opens upwards (coefficient of $a^2$ is positive), the expression is greater than zero *outside* the roots:

$$a < -2 \quad \text{or} \quad a > 3.5$$

Because $a$ represents a length, we must have $a > 0$, so we discard the negative region. This gives our lower bound:

$$a > 3.5$$

ti84.instructions: Graph Y1 = 2x^2 - 3x - 14 and find zero

steps.title: Apply triangle inequality
steps.content:
For a triangle to exist, the sum of any two side lengths must be greater than the third side. This gives us the upper bound for $a$.

Comparing the sum of the known sides to $a$:

$$\begin{aligned} AC + AB &> BC \\ 3 + 4 &> a \\ 7 &> a \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, $a < 7$.

steps.title: State the final range
steps.content:
Combining the results from the cosine condition ($a > 3.5$) and the triangle inequality ($a < 7$), the range of possible values for $a$ is:

$$3.5 < a < 7$$

Or in interval notation:

$$a \in (\frac{7}{2}, 7)$$

steps.title: Use the Cosine Rule
steps.content:
To define the relationship between the side lengths and the angle $\theta$, we use the cosine rule from the data booklet. The angle $\theta$ (at vertex C) is opposite side $c = 4$.

$$c^{2}=a^{2}+b^{2}-2ab\cos C$$

Substitute the values from the triangle ($AB=4, AC=3, BC=a$):

$$\begin{aligned} 4^2 &= 3^2 + a^2 - 2(3)(a)\cos \theta \\ 16 &= 9 + a^2 - 6a\cos \theta \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Isolate cos θ
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to make $\cos \theta$ the subject so we can plot it as a function of $a$.

$$\begin{aligned} 6a\cos \theta &= a^2 + 9 - 16 \\ 6a\cos \theta &= a^2 - 7 \\ \cos \theta &= \frac{a^2 - 7}{6a} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Plot the functions
steps.content:
We are given the condition $\cos \theta > 0.25$. To solve this graphically, treat $a$ as the variable $x$ and plot the function for $\cos \theta$ and the constant line $y=0.25$.

Plot these two functions:
1.  $$y_1 = \frac{x^2 - 7}{6x}$$
2.  $$y_2 = 0.25$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Graph menu.
2. Enter f1(x) = (x^2 - 7) / (6x).
3. Enter f2(x) = 0.25.
4. Set view window for x from 0 to 10 and y from -1.5 to 1.5.
5. Draw the graph.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = (X^2 - 7) / (6X).
3. Enter Y2 = 0.25.
4. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=10, Ymin=-1, Ymax=1 (since cos θ is between -1 and 1).
5. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions:
1. Type y = (x^2 - 7) / (6x).
2. Type y = 0.25.
3. Look for the intersection point.

calculator.description: Find the intersection of the curve and the line

steps.title: Find intersection point
steps.content:
Use your calculator's intersection tool to find the value of $a$ (or $x$) where the two graphs meet.

The intersection occurs at:
$$a = 3.5$$

This is the critical value where $\cos \theta = 0.25$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Select G-Solve (or Analysis).
2. Select ISCT (Intersection).
3. The calculator displays x = 3.5.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC).
2. Select 5: intersect.
3. Press [ENTER] on the first curve, [ENTER] on the second curve, and [ENTER] for Guess.
4. The calculator displays X=3.5.

desmos.instructions: Click on the point where the red curve crosses the blue line. It shows (3.5, 0.25).

steps.title: Determine lower bound
steps.content:
Examine the graph. The curve $y = \frac{x^2 - 7}{6x}$ is increasing for positive $x$. We need the part where the curve is **above** the line $y=0.25$ (since $\cos \theta > 0.25$).

Looking at the graph, the $y$-values are greater than $0.25$ when $x$ is greater than the intersection point.

Therefore, we need:
$$a > 3.5$$

steps.title: Apply geometric constraints
steps.content:
For a triangle to exist, the sum of any two sides must be greater than the third side (Triangle Inequality). Specifically, the two shorter sides must sum to more than the longest side.

$$\begin{aligned} AC + BC > AB &\Rightarrow 3 + a > 4 \Rightarrow a > 1 \\ AC + AB > BC &\Rightarrow 3 + 4 > a \Rightarrow a < 7 \end{aligned}$$

The second inequality provides an upper limit for $a$. If $a \geq 7$, the sides $3$ and $4$ cannot form a closed triangle with side $a$ (it would be a flat line or impossible).

Combining this with our graphical result ($a > 3.5$):

steps.title: State the range
steps.content:
The possible values for $a$ are bounded by the intersection point ($3.5$) and the triangle inequality limit ($7$).

$$3.5 < a < 7$$

Or in interval notation:
$$a \in (3.5, 7)$$

The angles of elevation of the balloon are \(29^\circ\) from \(A\) and \(41^\circ\) from \(B\).

parts.content: Draw a fully-labelled 3-D diagram showing \(A,B,P_0,P\) and all the given angles.
parts.markscheme:
![Diagram showing tetrahedron ABP0P](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/8851c1d8-6e01-4b69-b2f5-de299c25cbb7.png)

- Triangle \(ABP_0\) with \(AB=110\text{ m}\), \(\angle AP_0B=50^\circ\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Vertical line \(PP_0\) and angles \(29^\circ\) and \(41^\circ\) correctly labelled. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the 3D geometry
steps.content:
To solve this problem using the **Constructing the 3D Diagram** approach, we need to break the shape into two parts:
1. A horizontal base triangle $ABP_0$ on the ground.
2. A vertical height $PP_0$ representing the balloon's position.

This creates a tetrahedron (a triangular pyramid) where $P_0$ is the foot of the perpendicular from $P$. This relates to **Topic 3: Geometry & Trigonometry**, specifically angles of elevation and 3D shapes.

steps.title: Sketch the horizontal base
steps.content:
Draw the triangle $ABP_0$ to represent the ground level.

*   Label the vertices $A$, $B$, and $P_0$.
*   Label the side $AB$ with its length: $110\text{ m}$.
*   Label the angle at $P_0$ inside the triangle: $\angle AP_0B = 50^\circ$.

highlights.text: The ground distance between \(A\) and \(B\) is 110 m

steps.title: Add the vertical height
steps.content:
Since the balloon is directly above $P_0$, draw a vertical line segment upwards from $P_0$ and label the top point $P$.

Then, connect point $P$ to points $A$ and $B$ to form the cables holding the balloon. This creates two right-angled vertical triangles: $\triangle APP_0$ and $\triangle BPP_0$.

highlights.text: The balloon is directly above its projection \(P_0\)

steps.title: Label angles of elevation
steps.content:
The angle of elevation is the angle between the horizontal ground and the line of sight looking up at the balloon.

*   At point $A$, label the angle formed between the ground line $AP_0$ and the cable $AP$ as $29^\circ$.
*   At point $B$, label the angle formed between the ground line $BP_0$ and the cable $BP$ as $41^\circ$.

highlights.text: angles of elevation of the balloon are \(29^\circ\) from \(A\)

steps.title: Final check
steps.content:
Verify your diagram matches the requirements for full marks:

1.  **Triangle $ABP_0$**: Labeled with $AB=110$ and $\angle AP_0B = 50^\circ$. [M1]
2.  **Vertical Height $PP_0$**: Shows $P$ above $P_0$ with angles $29^\circ$ (at $A$) and $41^\circ$ (at $B$) correctly placed. [A1]

Your final diagram should look like a pyramid with a triangular base, clearly showing the 3D nature of the problem.

parts.content:
Let \(AP_0=x\) m and \(BP_0=y\) m.
Show that
\[
x\tan29^\circ = y\tan41^\circ.
\]
parts.markscheme:
- From right triangles: \(h = x\tan29^\circ\), \(h = y\tan41^\circ\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Hence \(x\tan29^\circ = y\tan41^\circ\). <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the common height
steps.content:
Let $h$ be the vertical height of the balloon $P$ above the ground point $P_0$. This height is the common vertical side shared by the two right-angled triangles formed by the cables and the ground.

Since $P$ is directly above $P_0$, the line $PP_0$ is perpendicular to the ground, creating right angles at $P_0$.

highlights.text: balloon is directly above its projection

steps.title: Find height from triangle A
steps.content:
Consider the right-angled triangle formed by points $A$, $P_0$, and $P$. We know the angle of elevation from $A$ is $29^\circ$ and the adjacent side $AP_0 = x$.

Using the tangent ratio ($\text{tan} = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}$):

$$\tan 29^\circ = \frac{h}{x}$$

Rearranging for $h$:

$$h = x \tan 29^\circ$$

highlights.text: angles of elevation of the balloon are \(29^\circ\) from \(A\)

steps.title: Find height from triangle B
steps.content:
Now consider the right-angled triangle formed by points $B$, $P_0$, and $P$. The angle of elevation from $B$ is $41^\circ$ and the adjacent side $BP_0 = y$.

Using the tangent ratio again:

$$\tan 41^\circ = \frac{h}{y}$$

Rearranging for $h$:

$$h = y \tan 41^\circ$$

steps.title: Equate the heights
steps.content:
Since both expressions represent the same height $h$, we can set them equal to each other:

$$x \tan 29^\circ = y \tan 41^\circ$$

This confirms the required relationship.

parts.content: Using the cosine rule in triangle \(ABP_0\), write an equation relating \(x\) and \(y\).
parts.markscheme:
- \(110^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2xy\cos50^\circ\). <marks>M1 A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify triangle components
steps.content:
First, we identify the relevant information for triangle $ABP_0$. The problem asks us to relate sides $x$ and $y$ using the cosine rule.

From the question, we know:
- The angle at $P_0$ is given as $\angle AP_0B = 50^\circ$.
- The side opposite this angle is the distance $AB$, which is $110$ m.
- The other two sides meeting at $P_0$ are $AP_0 = x$ and $BP_0 = y$.

highlights.text: The ground distance between A and B is 110 m

steps.title: Select the Cosine Rule
steps.content: Since we have two sides ($x, y$) and the included angle ($50^\circ$) relating to the opposite side ($110$), we use the cosine rule from the **Geometry & Trigonometry** section of the data booklet.

steps.title: Substitute values into formula
steps.content:
We substitute our specific values into the formula:
- $c = 110$ (the side opposite the angle)
- $a = x$ and $b = y$ (the adjacent sides)
- $C = 50^\circ$ (the included angle)

$$\begin{aligned} 110^2 &= x^2 + y^2 - 2(x)(y)\cos 50^\circ \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.content:
This matches the required form for the equation relating $x$ and $y$.

$$110^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2xy\cos50^\circ$$

Note: $110^2$ can be calculated as $12100$, but the markscheme often accepts the unsimplified square form.

ti84.instructions: Type 110^2 and press ENTER

parts.content: Find the height \(h\) of the balloon, correct to the nearest metre.
parts.markscheme:
- From (b): \(y = \dfrac{x\tan29^\circ}{\tan41^\circ}\). Substitute into (c):
  \(110^2 = x^2 + \Bigl(\dfrac{x\tan29^\circ}{\tan41^\circ}\Bigr)^2
            -2x\Bigl(\dfrac{x\tan29^\circ}{\tan41^\circ}\Bigr)\cos50^\circ.\)
  So \(x \approx 143.6\text{ m}\), \(y \approx 91.6\text{ m}\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Height \(h = x\tan29^\circ \approx 143.6\tan29^\circ \approx 79.6\text{ m}\).
  So \(h \approx 80\text{ m}\). <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Express sides in terms of h
steps.content:
First, we express the horizontal distances $AP_0$ and $BP_0$ in terms of the height $h$. From the right-angled triangles formed by the balloon's height and the ground:

$$\tan 29^\circ = \frac{h}{AP_0} \implies AP_0 = \frac{h}{\tan 29^\circ} = h \cot 29^\circ$$

$$\tan 41^\circ = \frac{h}{BP_0} \implies BP_0 = \frac{h}{\tan 41^\circ} = h \cot 41^\circ$$

highlights.text: angles of elevation of the balloon are 29° from A and 41° from B

steps.title: Substitute into Cosine Rule
steps.content:
Next, we use the Cosine Rule on the horizontal triangle $\triangle AP_0B$ with side $AB = 110$ and angle $\angle AP_0B = 50^\circ$.

$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$$

Substituting our expressions for $AP_0$ and $BP_0$:

$$110^2 = (h \cot 29^\circ)^2 + (h \cot 41^\circ)^2 - 2(h \cot 29^\circ)(h \cot 41^\circ) \cos 50^\circ$$

highlights.text: distance between A and B is 110 m

steps.title: Solve for h
steps.content:
Factor out $h^2$ from the equation to isolate $h$:

$$110^2 = h^2 [\cot^2 29^\circ + \cot^2 41^\circ - 2 \cot 29^\circ \cot 41^\circ \cos 50^\circ]$$

$$h = \sqrt{\frac{110^2}{\cot^2 29^\circ + \cot^2 41^\circ - 2 \cot 29^\circ \cot 41^\circ \cos 50^\circ}}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression carefully using parenthesis for the denominator. Note that cot(x) is entered as 1/tan(x).

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
From the calculation:

$$h \approx 79.59 \text{ m}$$

Rounding to the nearest metre:

$$h = 80 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Define variables and relationships
steps.content:
Let $h$ be the height of the balloon ($PP_0$). Let $x$ be the horizontal distance $AP_0$ and $y$ be the horizontal distance $BP_0$.

Using the right-angled triangles formed by the balloon's height and the ground distances:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan 29^\circ &= \frac{h}{x} \implies h = x \tan 29^\circ \\ \tan 41^\circ &= \frac{h}{y} \implies h = y \tan 41^\circ \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: angles of elevation of the balloon are \(29^\circ\) from \(A\) and \(41^\circ\) from \(B\)

steps.title: Express y in terms of x
steps.content:
Since both expressions equal $h$, we can equate them to find a relationship between $x$ and $y$:

$$x \tan 29^\circ = y \tan 41^\circ$$

Solving for $y$:

$$y = x \frac{\tan 29^\circ}{\tan 41^\circ}$$

steps.title: Apply Cosine Rule
steps.content:
Now consider the triangle on the ground, $\triangle AP_0B$. We know the side $AB = 110\text{ m}$ and the angle $\angle AP_0B = 50^\circ$. Using the **Cosine rule** from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet:

$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$$

Substitute $c = 110$, $a = x$, $b = y$, and $C = 50^\circ$:

$$110^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2xy \cos 50^\circ$$

highlights.text: ground distance between \(A\) and \(B\) is 110 m

steps.title: Substitute and solve for x
steps.content:
Substitute $y = x \frac{\tan 29^\circ}{\tan 41^\circ}$ into the Cosine Rule equation and factor out $x^2$:

$$\begin{aligned} 110^2 &= x^2 + \left( x \frac{\tan 29^\circ}{\tan 41^\circ} \right)^2 \\ &\quad - 2x \left( x \frac{\tan 29^\circ}{\tan 41^\circ} \right) \cos 50^\circ \\ 110^2 &= x^2 \left[ 1 + \left( \frac{\tan 29^\circ}{\tan 41^\circ} \right)^2 - 2 \left( \frac{\tan 29^\circ}{\tan 41^\circ} \right) \cos 50^\circ \right] \end{aligned}$$

Use your calculator to evaluate the term in the brackets and solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 12100 &\approx x^2 [0.58685...] \\ x^2 &\approx \frac{12100}{0.58685...} \\ x &\approx 143.59 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Store tan(29)/tan(41) as k
2. Compute 1 + k^2 - 2*k*cos(50) as B
3. Compute sqrt(110^2 / B)

ti84.instructions:
1. Calculate the ratio k = tan(29)/tan(41)
2. Calculate the bracket term B = 1 + k² - 2*k*cos(50)
3. Calculate x = sqrt(110² / B)

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly to find x

calculator.description: Calculate the factor for x² and solve for x

steps.title: Calculate height
steps.content:
Now substitute $x \approx 143.59$ back into the first equation to find the height $h$:

$$\begin{aligned} h &= x \tan 29^\circ \\ h &\approx 143.59 \times \tan 29^\circ \\ h &\approx 79.6 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the previous answer by tan(29)

ti84.instructions: Multiply the previous answer by tan(29)

desmos.instructions: Multiply the value of x by tan(29)

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
Rounding to the nearest metre:

$$h \approx 80 \text{ m}$$

parts.content:
The projection \(P_0\) moves horizontally around a circle of radius 110 m centred at \(A\).
Find the angle subtended by the arc in radians and the length of the arc in metres when the bearing of \(P_0\) from \(A\) changes from \(020^\circ\) to \(170^\circ\).
parts.markscheme:
- Bearing change: \(\theta = 170^\circ - 20^\circ = 150^\circ = \dfrac{5\pi}{6}\text{ rad}.\) <marks>M1</marks>
- Arc length: \(s = 110\cdot\dfrac{5\pi}{6} = \dfrac{550\pi}{6} \approx 288.0\text{ m}\). <marks>A1 A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
First, we identify the key values provided in the question. The projection $P_0$ moves along a circular path centered at $A$ with a known radius. We are also given the starting and ending bearings.

*   Radius $r = 110 \text{ m}$ 
*   Initial bearing = $020^\circ$
*   Final bearing = $170^\circ$

highlights.text: bearing of P_0 from A changes from 020^\circ to 170^\circ

steps.title: Calculate the angle in degrees
steps.content:
Bearings are measured clockwise from North. The angle subtended by the arc at the center $A$ is simply the difference between the two bearings.

$$\theta_{\text{deg}} = 170^\circ - 20^\circ = 150^\circ$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate 170 - 20

steps.title: Convert degrees to radians
steps.content:
To use the standard arc length formula, the angle must be in radians. We convert degrees to radians by multiplying by $\frac{\pi}{180}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \theta &= 150 \times \frac{\pi}{180} \\ &= \frac{15\pi}{18} \\ &= \frac{5\pi}{6} \text{ radians} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate 150 * (pi/180)

steps.title: Calculate arc length
steps.content:
Now we calculate the arc length $l$ using the formula from the data booklet, where $r$ is the radius and $\theta$ is the angle in radians.

$$l = \theta r$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} l &= \frac{5\pi}{6} \times 110 \\ &= \frac{550\pi}{6} \\ &= \frac{275\pi}{3} \\ &\approx 288 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate 110 * (5*pi/6)

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The question asks for both the angle in radians and the length of the arc.

Angle: $$\frac{5\pi}{6} \text{ radians} \quad (\approx 2.62 \text{ rad})$$

Arc length: $$288 \text{ m} \quad (\text{to 3 s.f.})$$

steps.title: Calculate angle in degrees
steps.content:
First, we find the change in bearing to determine the angle subtended by the arc in degrees. The bearing changes from $020^\circ$ to $170^\circ$.

$$\theta_{\text{deg}} = 170^\circ - 20^\circ = 150^\circ$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 170 - 20

highlights.text: bearing of \(P_0\) from \(A\) changes from \(020^\circ\) to \(170^\circ\)

steps.title: Convert angle to radians
steps.content:
To answer the first part of the question, we convert this angle to radians by multiplying by $\frac{\pi}{180}$.

$$\theta_{\text{rad}} = 150 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{15\pi}{18} = \frac{5\pi}{6} \text{ rad}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply 150 by pi/180

steps.title: Calculate total circumference
steps.content:
Using the circle proportion approach, we first find the total circumference of the circle using the radius $r = 110\text{ m}$.

$$C = 2\pi r = 2\pi(110) = 220\pi \text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate 2 * pi * 110

steps.title: Calculate arc length fraction
steps.content:
The arc length $s$ is the fraction of the circumference corresponding to the angle in degrees.

$$s = \frac{\theta_{\text{deg}}}{360} \times C$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{150}{360} \times 220\pi \\ &= \frac{5}{12} \times 220\pi \\ &= \frac{1100\pi}{12} \\ &= \frac{275\pi}{3} \approx 288.0 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate (150/360) * 2 * pi * 110

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content: The angle subtended is $\frac{5\pi}{6}$ radians and the arc length is approximately $288.0$ m.

parts.content: If tensions are \(T_A = k|AP|\) and \(T_B = k|BP|\), find \(\dfrac{T_A}{T_B}\).
parts.markscheme:
- \(AP = \sqrt{x^2 + h^2} \approx \sqrt{143.6^2 + 79.6^2} \approx 164.2\text{ m}\).
  \(BP = \sqrt{91.6^2 + 79.6^2} \approx 121.3\text{ m}\). <marks>M1 M1</marks>
- \(\dfrac{T_A}{T_B} \approx \dfrac{164.2}{121.3} \approx 1.35.\) <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Set up the ratio
steps.content:
The question asks for the ratio of the tensions $\frac{T_A}{T_B}$. We are given that the tension is proportional to the length of the cable.

Since $k$ is a constant, it cancels out immediately:

$$\frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{k|AP|}{k|BP|} = \frac{|AP|}{|BP|}$$

steps.title: Express lengths using height
steps.content:
Let $h$ be the vertical height of the balloon $P$ above the ground point $P_0$. We can express the cable lengths $|AP|$ and $|BP|$ using the sine ratio $\sin \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$ in the right-angled vertical triangles.

For cable $A$ with elevation $29^\circ$:
$$\sin 29^\circ = \frac{h}{|AP|} \implies |AP| = \frac{h}{\sin 29^\circ}$$

For cable $B$ with elevation $41^\circ$:
$$\sin 41^\circ = \frac{h}{|BP|} \implies |BP| = \frac{h}{\sin 41^\circ}$$

highlights.text: angles of elevation of the balloon are 29^\circ from A and 41^\circ from B

steps.title: Substitute and cancel height
steps.content:
Now we substitute these expressions into our ratio equation. Since $h$ is in the numerator of both expressions, it will cancel out nicely:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{T_A}{T_B} &= \frac{|AP|}{|BP|} \\ &= \frac{\frac{h}{\sin 29^\circ}}{\frac{h}{\sin 41^\circ}} \\ &= \frac{h}{\sin 29^\circ} \times \frac{\sin 41^\circ}{h} \\ &= \frac{\sin 41^\circ}{\sin 29^\circ} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final ratio
steps.content:
Finally, evaluate the ratio using the sine values.

$$\frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{\sin 41^\circ}{\sin 29^\circ} \approx 1.353$$

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix mode, type the expression.

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is set to DEGREES. Enter the division.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The ratio of the tensions is approximately $1.35$.

$$\frac{T_A}{T_B} \approx 1.35$$

steps.title: Set up the tension ratio
steps.content:
The question asks for the ratio $\dfrac{T_A}{T_B}$. We are given expressions for the tensions in terms of the cable lengths and a constant $k$.

$$\begin{aligned} \dfrac{T_A}{T_B} &= \dfrac{k|AP|}{k|BP|} \\ &= \dfrac{|AP|}{|BP|} \end{aligned}$$

Notice that the constant $k$ cancels out, so we simply need to find the ratio of the cable lengths $|AP|$ and $|BP|$.

highlights.text: Tensions are T_A = k|AP| and T_B = k|BP|

steps.title: Calculate length of cable AP
steps.content:
Using the horizontal distance ($x \approx 143.6$) and height ($h \approx 79.6$) found in previous parts, we can calculate the length of cable $|AP|$ using the Pythagorean theorem:

$$|AP| = \sqrt{AP_0^2 + h^2}$$

Substituting the values:

$$|AP| = \sqrt{143.6^2 + 79.6^2} \approx 164.2 \text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the square root calculation:

steps.title: Calculate length of cable BP
steps.content:
Similarly, we calculate the length of cable $|BP|$ using the horizontal distance for B ($x_B \approx 91.6$) found in previous parts:

$$|BP| = \sqrt{BP_0^2 + h^2}$$

Substituting the values:

$$|BP| = \sqrt{91.6^2 + 79.6^2} \approx 121.3 \text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the square root calculation:

steps.title: Calculate final ratio
steps.content:
Now we divide the two lengths to find the ratio of the tensions:

$$\dfrac{T_A}{T_B} = \dfrac{164.2}{121.3} \approx 1.35$$

The ratio of the tensions is approximately **1.35**.

questions.specification:
In triangle $ABC$, point $D$ lies on $AC$ such that $DB$ bisects $\angle ABC$. Let $\angle ABD = \angle DBC = \theta$.
 
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_07_30_144a1a4c2d4db4411459g-16.jpg?height=483&width=1281&top_left_y=1012&top_left_x=419)

parts.content: Find the value of $x$ in the form $\frac{a}{b}$ where $a$ and $b$ are positive integers, given that $\sin \theta=\frac{4}{5}$ and the area of triangle $ABC$ is $3$.
parts.markscheme:
- Use the Pythagorean identity $\sin^2 \theta + \cos^2 \theta = 1$
- Substitute $\sin \theta = \frac{4}{5}$: $\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^2 + \cos^2 \theta = 1$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Solve for $\cos \theta$: $\cos^2 \theta = 1 - \frac{16}{25} = \frac{9}{25}$
- Determine $\cos \theta$: $\cos \theta = \frac{3}{5}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Use the area of a triangle formula: Area $ABC = \frac{1}{2}(AB)(BC) \sin(\angle ABC)$
- Substitute values: $3 = \frac{1}{2}(8)(x) \sin(2\theta)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Apply double angle formula: $3 = 4x(2 \sin \theta \cos \theta)$
- Substitute values: $3 = 8x\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)$
- Simplify: $3 = \frac{96x}{25}$
- Calculate the final answer: $x = \frac{75}{96} = \frac{25}{32}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the given information
steps.content:
We are given a triangle $ABC$ with side $AB = 8$ and side $BC = x$. The angle at $B$, $\angle ABC$, is split into two equal angles $\theta$, so the total angle is $\angle ABC = 2\theta$.

We know that:
- $\sin \theta = \frac{4}{5}$
- The area of triangle $ABC$ is $3$

Our goal is to find $x$ using the area formula and double angle identities.

steps.title: Calculate cos θ
steps.content:
First, we find $\cos \theta$ using the Pythagorean identity from the data booklet. Substituting $\sin \theta = \frac{4}{5}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos^2 \theta + \left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^2 &= 1 \\ \cos^2 \theta + \frac{16}{25} &= 1 \\ \cos^2 \theta &= 1 - \frac{16}{25} \\ \cos^2 \theta &= \frac{9}{25} \end{aligned}$$

Since $\angle ABC = 2\theta$ is an angle in a triangle, $\theta$ must be acute (otherwise $2\theta \ge 180^\circ$), so $\cos \theta$ is positive:

$$\cos \theta = \frac{3}{5}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate square root of 1 minus (4/5)^2

calculator.description: Verify the calculation for cos theta

steps.title: Set up area equation
steps.content:
Use the area formula for triangle $ABC$. We know sides $AB=8$, $BC=x$, and the included angle is $2\theta$.

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}(AB)(BC)\sin(2\theta)$$

Substituting the known values:

$$3 = \frac{1}{2}(8)(x)\sin(2\theta)$$

$$3 = 4x \sin(2\theta)$$

steps.title: Apply double angle identity
steps.content:
Now we substitute the double angle formula for $\sin 2\theta$ into our equation. This allows us to use the values of $\sin \theta$ and $\cos \theta$ we found earlier.

$$3 = 4x (2 \sin \theta \cos \theta)$$

Substitute $\sin \theta = \frac{4}{5}$ and $\cos \theta = \frac{3}{5}$:

$$3 = 8x \left(\frac{4}{5}\right)\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
Simplify the equation to solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 3 &= 8x \left(\frac{12}{25}\right) \\ 3 &= \frac{96x}{25} \\ 75 &= 96x \\ x &= \frac{75}{96} \end{aligned}$$

Finally, we simplify the fraction by dividing numerator and denominator by their greatest common divisor, which is 3:

$$x = \frac{25}{32}$$

steps.title: Analyze diagram and formula
steps.content:
From the provided diagram and question, we identify the following components:
- Side $AB = 8$ (given in diagram)
- Side $BC = x$
- The angle between them is $\angle ABC$. Since $DB$ bisects this angle and $\angle ABD = \theta$, the total angle is $\angle ABC = 2\theta$.

We need to find $x$ using the area information. We refer to the **Geometry & Trigonometry** section of the data booklet for the area of a triangle formula.

steps.title: Calculate angle theta
steps.content: We are given $\sin \theta = \frac{4}{5}$. Using the GDC, we can find the numerical value of $\theta$ using the inverse sine function. This value will be used directly in our next step.

ti84.instructions: Calculate inverse sine of 4/5. Ensure you are in Degree mode for easier interpretation, though Radians work too.

steps.title: Setup area equation
steps.content:
Now we substitute our values into the area formula $\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}ab\sin C$:

- $\text{Area} = 3$
- $a = 8$
- $b = x$
- $C = 2\theta$

This gives us the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 3 &= \frac{1}{2}(8)(x)\sin(2\theta) \\ 3 &= 4x\sin(2\theta) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to isolate $x$:
$$x = \frac{3}{4\sin(2\theta)}$$

Use your GDC to compute this value by substituting the $\theta$ we found in step 1. Note that we multiply the angle by 2 inside the sine function.

questions.specification: A ship sails from point $P$ on a bearing of $310^\circ$ for $k \text{ km}$ to point $Q$. It then changes course and sails on a bearing of $225^\circ$ for $10 \text{ km}$ to point $R$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.3—Angles of elevation and depression, bearings > Question Type 2: Exploring more complicated cases
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Exploring more complicated cases
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The problem is a bearings/navigation scenario (ship sails on given bearings with distances) requiring forming angles from bearings and using triangle relationships to express PR in terms of k and then solve for k.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.2—2d and 3d trig, sine rule, cosine rule, area > Question Type 4: Using cosine rule to find the 3rd side
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Using cosine rule to find the 3rd side
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) and (b) naturally use the cosine rule on triangle PQR to relate PR, PQ=k, QR=10, and the included angle derived from bearings, then solve for k given PR=15.

parts.content:
Find the distance $PR$ in terms of $k$.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/8e1518b4-33e3-45b5-97f7-62e6a76955ac.png)
parts.markscheme:
- Find the reverse bearing $Q\to P$: $310^\circ+180^\circ=490^\circ\equiv130^\circ$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the included angle at $Q$: $\angle PQR=|225^\circ-130^\circ|=95^\circ$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Apply the cosine rule with $QP=k$, $QR=10$ and included angle $95^\circ$:
  $$PR=\sqrt{k^2+10^2-2(k)(10)\cos95^\circ}=\sqrt{k^2+100-20k\cos95^\circ}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: If $PR = 15 \text{ km}$, find the exact value of $k$ where your answer can include $\cos\alpha^\circ$ for some $\alpha\in(0,360)$.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute $PR=15$ into the cosine rule expression:
  $$15 = \sqrt{k^2 + 10^2 - 20k\cos95^\circ}$$
- Square both sides and rearrange to form a quadratic equation in terms of $k$:
  $$225 = k^2 - 20k\cos95^\circ + 100 \implies k^2 - 20k\cos95^\circ - 125 = 0$$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Solve for $k$ using the quadratic formula:
  $$ \begin{aligned} k &= \frac{20\cos95^\circ \pm \sqrt{(-20\cos95^\circ)^2 - 4(1)(-125)}}{2} \\ &= \frac{20\cos95^\circ \pm \sqrt{400\cos^2 95^\circ + 500}}{2} \\ &= 10\cos95^\circ \pm \sqrt{100\cos^2 95^\circ + 125} \end{aligned} $$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Reject the negative solution since $k$ is a length and must be positive. Note that $\cos95^\circ < 0$. <marks>R1</marks>
- State the final exact value:
  $$k = 10\cos95^\circ + \sqrt{100\cos^2 95^\circ + 125}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

questions.specification: Given each of the following triangles, use the cosine rule to find the size of angle \( A \). Give your answers to one decimal place.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.2—2d and 3d trig, sine rule, cosine rule, area > Question Type 5: Using cosine rule to find all the angles between sides
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 5: Using cosine rule to find all the angles between sides
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly instructs using the cosine rule to find angle A given three side lengths in each triangle; this matches the cosine rule application for finding angles.

parts.content: In triangle \(ABC\), we have that \( a = 7 \, \text{cm} \), \( b = 8 \, \text{cm} \), and \( c = 11 \, \text{cm} \).
parts.markscheme:
- Using cosine rule: $\cos A = \frac{b^2 + c^2 - a^2}{2bc}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substituting values: $\cos A = \frac{8^2 + 11^2 - 7^2}{2 \times 8 \times 11}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- $\cos A = \frac{64 + 121 - 49}{176} = \frac{136}{176} = 0.7727...$ <marks>A1</marks>

- $A = \cos^{-1}(0.7727...) = 39.4^\circ$ (to 1 d.p.) <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<examTip>Remember to give your final answer to 1 decimal place as specified in the question</examTip>

steps.title: Identify given values and formula
steps.content:
To find angle $A$, we first identify the given side lengths from the question: $a = 7$, $b = 8$, and $c = 11$. 

The standard Cosine Rule is provided in your data booklet. For angle $A$, we use the rearranged version to isolate $\cos A$:

$$\cos A = \frac{b^2 + c^2 - a^2}{2bc}$$

steps.title: Substitute values into the formula
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our known values into the rearranged formula to find the value of $\cos A$:

$$\cos A = \frac{8^2 + 11^2 - 7^2}{2 \times 8 \times 11}$$

steps.title: Calculate the cosine value
steps.content:
Simplify the numerator and the denominator:

$$\cos A = \frac{64 + 121 - 49}{176}$$

$$\cos A = \frac{136}{176}$$

Converting this to a decimal gives us:

$$\cos A \approx 0.7727...$$

ti84.instructions: Enter (8^2 + 11^2 - 7^2) / (2 * 8 * 11) and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the fraction to get cos A.

steps.title: Solve for angle A
steps.content:
To find the angle $A$, take the inverse cosine ($\arccos$) of the result. Make sure your calculator is set to **Degree mode**.

$$A = \cos^{-1}(0.7727...)$$

$$A \approx 39.4005...^\circ$$

gdc.instructions: Use the inverse cosine function: cos⁻¹(136/176). Ensure the calculator is in degree mode.

ti84.instructions: Press 2nd then COS, then enter 136/176. Press ENTER. Ensure mode is DEGREE.

desmos.instructions: Type arccos(136/176). Click the wrench icon to ensure 'Degrees' is selected.

calculator.description: Find the inverse cosine of the result in degrees.

steps.title: Final answer with rounding
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer to **one decimal place**. 

$$A = 39.4^\circ$$

steps.title: Identify the objective
steps.content:
We need to find the size of angle $A$ in triangle $ABC$. We are given the side lengths:
- $a = 7 \text{ cm}$
- $b = 8 \text{ cm}$
- $c = 11 \text{ cm}$

This topic is covered in **SL 3.2: Sine and Cosine Rule**, which builds on basic trigonometry.

steps.title: Select the formula
steps.content:
From your IB Data Booklet, the Cosine Rule is provided. To find angle $A$, we use the version of the formula where the side opposite the angle ($a$) is on the left side:

$$a^2 = b^2 + c^2 - 2bc \cos A$$

steps.title: Substitute the known values
steps.content:
Now, we plug the values $a = 7$, $b = 8$, and $c = 11$ into the formula:

$$7^2 = 8^2 + 11^2 - 2(8)(11) \cos A$$

steps.title: Simplify the numerical terms
steps.content:
Calculate the squares and the product of the constants to simplify the equation:

$$49 = 64 + 121 - 176 \cos A$$

$$49 = 185 - 176 \cos A$$

steps.title: Isolate the cosine term
steps.content:
We need to get $\cos A$ by itself. First, subtract 185 from both sides:

$$49 - 185 = -176 \cos A$$

$$-136 = -176 \cos A$$

Next, divide both sides by -176:

$$\cos A = \frac{-136}{-176}$$

$$\cos A = \frac{136}{176}$$

steps.title: Solve for the angle
steps.content:
To find $A$, take the inverse cosine (arccos) of the fraction. This step is where you use your calculator.

$$A = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{136}{176}\right)$$

$$A \approx 39.363...^\circ$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Enter the run-matrix menu.
2. Ensure the angle unit is set to degrees in [SHIFT] [SET UP].
3. Use the [SHIFT] [COS] key to enter acos.
4. Type (136 ÷ 176) and press [EXE].

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] [COS] to get cos⁻¹.
2. Type (136 / 176).
3. Ensure your calculator is in DEGREE mode (Press [MODE], highlight 'DEGREE', and press [ENTER]).
4. Press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
1. Set the calculator to Degree mode using the wrench icon.
2. Type 'arccos(136/176)' or use the keypad functions.

calculator.description: Calculate the angle using the inverse cosine function.

steps.title: Final answer and rounding
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer to **one decimal place**.

$$A = 39.4^\circ$$

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content: In this problem, we are given the lengths of all three sides of triangle $ABC$: $a = 7\text{ cm}$, $b = 8\text{ cm}$, and $c = 11\text{ cm}$. Since we know three sides and want to find an angle, we use the **Cosine Rule**. This concept is found in **Topic 3.2: Geometry and Trigonometry**.

steps.title: Select the Cosine Rule
steps.content:
Look at the **Trigonometry** section of your IB Data Booklet. The Cosine Rule is provided as:

$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$$

To find angle $A$, we can rewrite this formula by swapping the letters to:

$$a^2 = b^2 + c^2 - 2bc \cos A$$

steps.title: Substitute values into equation
steps.content:
Now, substitute the known side lengths into the formula:

$$7^2 = 8^2 + 11^2 - 2(8)(11) \cos A$$

Simplifying the numerical parts, we get:

$$49 = 64 + 121 - 176 \cos A$$

steps.title: Solve using GDC
steps.content:
Instead of rearranging the equation manually, you can use the **Numerical Solver** function on your GDC. 

Define your variable (angle $A$) as $x$ and enter the equation into the solver. **Crucial:** Ensure your calculator is set to **Degree mode** before you begin.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the 'Equation' or 'Solver' menu. 
2. Enter the equation: 7^2 = 8^2 + 11^2 - 2*8*11*cos(x). 
3. Press F6 (SOLVE).

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MATH] and select 'B:Numeric Solver...'. 
2. In E1, enter: 7^2. 
3. In E2, enter: 8^2 + 11^2 - 2*8*11*cos(x). 
4. Press [OK] (Graph), then [SOLVE] (Graph).

desmos.instructions:
1. Type the equation: 7^2 = 8^2 + 11^2 - 2(8)(11)cos(x). 
2. Adjust the window to see the value of x where the equation is satisfied (look for intersections with y=49).

calculator.description: Solving for angle A using the Numerical Solver function.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Your calculator will show that $A \approx 39.4005...^\circ$. 

Following the instructions in the question, we round our answer to **one decimal place**.

parts.content: In triangle \(ABC\), \( a = 10 \, \text{cm} \), \( b = 12 \, \text{cm} \), and \( c = 15 \, \text{cm} \).
parts.markscheme:
- Using cosine rule: $\cos A = \frac{b^2 + c^2 - a^2}{2bc}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substituting values: $\cos A = \frac{12^2 + 15^2 - 10^2}{2 \times 12 \times 15}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- $\cos A = \frac{144 + 225 - 100}{360} = \frac{269}{360} = 0.7472...$ <marks>A1</marks>

- $A = \cos^{-1}(0.7472...) = 41.6^\circ$ (to 1 d.p.) <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<examTip>Remember to give your final answer to 1 decimal place as specified in the question</examTip>

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content:
First, we identify the side lengths of the triangle from the question:
- Side opposite angle $A$: $a = 10 \, \text{cm}$
- Adjacent side 1: $b = 12 \, \text{cm}$
- Adjacent side 2: $c = 15 \, \text{cm}$

steps.title: Select the rearranged cosine rule
steps.content:
The **Cosine Rule** is used to find an angle when all three sides are known. The data booklet gives the formula for a side length, but for this method, we use the rearranged version to make $\cos A$ the subject.

Data booklet formula:
$$c^{2}=a^{2}+b^{2}-2ab\cos C$$

Rearranged for Angle $A$:
$$\cos A = \frac{b^2 + c^2 - a^2}{2bc}$$

steps.title: Substitute the values
steps.content:
Substitute the side lengths $a = 10$, $b = 12$, and $c = 15$ into the rearranged formula:

$$\cos A = \frac{12^2 + 15^2 - 10^2}{2(12)(15)}$$

steps.title: Calculate the fraction
steps.content:
Calculate the numerator and denominator separately or enter the fraction into your calculator to find the value of $\cos A$:

$$\cos A = \frac{144 + 225 - 100}{360}$$

$$\cos A = \frac{269}{360}$$

$$\cos A \approx 0.7472...$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the numerator and denominator as a fraction

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the fraction

steps.title: Solve for angle A
steps.content:
To find angle $A$, take the inverse cosine ($\cos^{-1}$) of the value found in the previous step.

$$A = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{269}{360}\right)$$

$$A \approx 41.649...^\circ$$

gdc.instructions: Use the arccos function on the previous answer

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [COS], then [2nd] [Ans] to use the previous result

calculator.description: Find the angle using inverse cosine (ensure degree mode)

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer to **one decimal place**. 

$$A = 41.6^\circ$$

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
We are given the three side lengths of triangle $ABC$ and need to find the angle $A$. This is a classic case for using the **Cosine Rule**.

Given values:
- $a = 10 \text{ cm}$ (opposite to angle $A$)
- $b = 12 \text{ cm}$
- $c = 15 \text{ cm}$

This relates to **SL 3.2** in the syllabus (finding angles using cosine rule).

steps.title: Select the cosine rule formula
steps.content:
From the **Geometry & Trigonometry** section of the data booklet, we select the cosine rule formula. While the standard formula is written for angle $C$, we can adapt it for angle $A$ by swapping the variables so that side $a$ is the subject.

Standard formula:
$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$$

Adapted for angle $A$:
$$a^2 = b^2 + c^2 - 2bc \cos A$$

steps.title: Substitute the values
steps.content:
Substitute the side lengths $a=10$, $b=12$, and $c=15$ directly into the formula:

$$10^2 = 12^2 + 15^2 - 2(12)(15) \cos A$$

steps.title: Evaluate the squares and products
steps.content:
Calculate the numeric values for the squared terms and the coefficient of $\cos A$:

$$100 = 144 + 225 - 360 \cos A$$

$$100 = 369 - 360 \cos A$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate the terms separately:

gdc.instructions: Calculate the coefficient of cos A:

ti84.instructions: Calculate the coefficient of cos A:

desmos.instructions: Calculate the coefficient of cos A:

steps.title: Rearrange to solve for cos A
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to isolate $\cos A$. Subtract 369 from both sides, then divide by -360 (or move the cosine term to the left and the constant to the right).

$$360 \cos A = 369 - 100$$

$$360 \cos A = 269$$

$$\cos A = \frac{269}{360}$$

steps.title: Find angle A
steps.content:
Use the inverse cosine function ($\cos^{-1}$ or $\arccos$) to find the angle $A$.

$$A = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{269}{360}\right)$$

gdc.instructions: Use arccos function. Ensure mode is Degree.

desmos.instructions: Type arccos(269/360). Click wrench icon to ensure Degrees mode.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives:

$$A \approx 41.64967...^{\circ}$$

The question asks for the answer to **one decimal place**.

$$A = 41.6^{\circ}$$

questions.specification:
A surveillance drone $P$ is hovering above a city square and is connected by safety cables to points $A$ and $B$ on rooftops at the same level.  
The distance between $A$ and $B$ is 95 m. The drone is vertically above $P_0$, and $\angle AP_0B = 65^\circ$ in the horizontal triangle.

At a particular moment, the angle of elevation of the drone is $24^\circ$ from $A$ and $39^\circ$ from $B$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.3—Angles of elevation and depression, bearings > Question Type 3: Applying values of elevation and depression
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Applying values of elevation and depression
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a–d) use angles of elevation from A and B to relate horizontal distances x,y and find the drone height via tan in right triangles.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.2—2d and 3d trig, sine rule, cosine rule, area > Question Type 4: Using cosine rule to find the 3rd side
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Using cosine rule to find the 3rd side
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) explicitly requires the cosine rule in triangle ABP0 to relate x and y given AB and included angle ∠AP0B.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.3—Angles of elevation and depression, bearings > Question Type 1: Finding angles using bearings
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Finding angles using bearings
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) involves bearings (bearing of P0 from A changing from 060° to 180°) and finding the associated angle change.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.4—Circle, radians, arcs, sectors > Question Type 1: Converting from degrees to radians
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Converting from degrees to radians
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) asks for the bearing change in radians, requiring degree-to-radian conversion.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Calculating the arc length given a radius and specific angle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) asks for the arc length traveled on a circle of radius 95 m given the angle change.

parts.content: Draw a fully-labelled 3-D diagram showing $A,B,P_0,P$ and all known angles.
parts.markscheme:
- Draw triangle $ABP_0$ with $AB=95\text{ m}$ and $\angle AP_0B=65^\circ$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Draw vertical line $PP_0$ and indicate angles of elevation $24^\circ$ and $39^\circ$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Construct the horizontal base
steps.content:
First, sketch the horizontal triangle $ABP_0$ in perspective (looking somewhat flattened). This triangle represents the layout on the ground/rooftop level.

Label the vertices $A$, $B$, and $P_0$. Add the known measurements for this horizontal plane:
- Label the side connecting the rooftops: $AB = 95\text{ m}$.
- Label the horizontal angle at $P_0$: $\angle AP_0B = 65^\circ$.

steps.title: Add the vertical drone position
steps.content:
Next, draw a vertical line rising straight up from $P_0$ to a point $P$. This represents the drone hovering above the square.

- The line $PP_0$ is perpendicular to the horizontal plane $ABP_0$.
- This creates two right-angled vertical triangles: $\triangle PP_0A$ and $\triangle PP_0B$.

steps.title: Label angles of elevation
steps.content:
Finally, connect the drone $P$ to points $A$ and $B$ to show the lines of sight. The angle of elevation is the angle between the horizontal line on the base and the line of sight to the drone.

- In vertical triangle $\triangle PP_0A$, label the angle at $A$: $\angle PAP_0 = 24^\circ$.
- In vertical triangle $\triangle PP_0B$, label the angle at $B$: $\angle PBP_0 = 39^\circ$.

Your diagram should now show a pyramid-like shape with base $ABP_0$ and apex $P$.

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the drone is $24^\circ$ from $A$

parts.content:
Let $AP_0=x$ m and $BP_0=y$ m.  
Show that  
$$
x\tan24^\circ = y\tan39^\circ.
$$
parts.markscheme:
- Express height $h$ using right triangles: $h = x\tan24^\circ$ and $h = y\tan39^\circ$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Equate expressions for $h$: $x\tan24^\circ = y\tan39^\circ$ <marks>AG</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the common height
steps.content:
To connect the information from the two rooftops, we need to focus on the common vertical height of the drone. Let $h$ be the vertical height of the drone $P$ above the point $P_0$. 

Both triangles $\triangle AP_0P$ and $\triangle BP_0P$ share this vertical side $PP_0 = h$.

steps.title: Express height in triangle A
steps.content:
In the vertical right-angled triangle formed by the drone and point A, we know the **angle of elevation** is $24^\circ$ and the adjacent side is $x$. 

Using the definition $$\tan \theta = \frac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}}$$, we can express the height $h$ as:

$$\tan 24^\circ = \frac{h}{x} \implies h = x\tan 24^\circ$$

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the drone is $24^\circ$ from $A$

steps.title: Express height in triangle B
steps.content:
Similarly, looking at the triangle formed by the drone and point B, the **angle of elevation** is $39^\circ$ and the adjacent side is $y$. 

We express the same height $h$ as:

$$\tan 39^\circ = \frac{h}{y} \implies h = y\tan 39^\circ$$

steps.title: Equate the expressions
steps.content:
Since both expressions represent the same height $h$, we can equate them to find the relationship between $x$ and $y$:

$$x\tan 24^\circ = y\tan 39^\circ$$

steps.title: Define the vertical height
steps.content: Let $h$ be the vertical height of the drone $P$ above the point $P_0$. We will relate the horizontal distances $x$ and $y$ to this common height $h$ using the two vertical right-angled triangles $\triangle APP_0$ and $\triangle BPP_0$.

steps.title: Apply sine rule to triangle APP₀
steps.content:
In the right-angled triangle $\triangle APP_0$, the angle of elevation at $A$ is $24^\circ$. The angle at the top vertex $P$ is the complement of the elevation:

$$90^\circ - 24^\circ = 66^\circ$$

We apply the sine rule relating side $h$ (opposite $24^\circ$) and side $x$ (opposite $66^\circ$):

$$\frac{h}{\sin 24^\circ} = \frac{x}{\sin 66^\circ}$$

steps.title: Express h in terms of x
steps.content:
Rearranging the equation for $h$ and using the identity $\sin(90^\circ - \theta) = \cos \theta$:

$$h = \frac{x \sin 24^\circ}{\sin 66^\circ} = \frac{x \sin 24^\circ}{\cos 24^\circ}$$

Since $\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta} = \tan \theta$, this simplifies to:

$$h = x \tan 24^\circ$$

steps.title: Apply sine rule to triangle BPP₀
steps.content:
Similarly, in the right-angled triangle $\triangle BPP_0$, the angle of elevation at $B$ is $39^\circ$. The angle at vertex $P$ is:

$$90^\circ - 39^\circ = 51^\circ$$

Applying the sine rule to sides $h$ and $y$:

$$\frac{h}{\sin 39^\circ} = \frac{y}{\sin 51^\circ}$$

steps.title: Express h in terms of y
steps.content:
Rearranging for $h$ and simplifying using the complementary angle identity:

$$h = \frac{y \sin 39^\circ}{\sin 51^\circ} = \frac{y \sin 39^\circ}{\cos 39^\circ}$$

$$h = y \tan 39^\circ$$

steps.title: Equate the expressions
steps.content:
Since both expressions represent the same vertical height $h$, we equate them to show the required relationship:

$$x \tan 24^\circ = y \tan 39^\circ$$

parts.content: Using the cosine rule in triangle $ABP_0$, write an equation relating $x$ and $y$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the cosine rule equation: $95^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2xy\cos65^\circ$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify triangle components
steps.content:
First, we identify the sides and angles in the horizontal triangle $ABP_0$ described in the question. We are given the following information:

*   Side $AB = 95$ (the side opposite the known angle)
*   Side $AP_0 = x$
*   Side $BP_0 = y$
*   Included angle $\angle AP_0B = 65^\circ$

This setup relates three sides and one included angle, which suggests using the cosine rule.

steps.title: Select the cosine rule formula
steps.content:
Since we need to relate the three sides and the included angle, we use the cosine rule from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet. 

$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab\cos C$$

In this formula, $C$ represents the angle, and $c$ represents the side length opposite that angle.

steps.title: Substitute values into the formula
steps.content:
We substitute our specific values into the formula:

*   Replace $c$ with the side opposite the angle: $95$
*   Replace $C$ with the angle: $65^\circ$
*   Replace $a$ and $b$ with the adjacent sides: $x$ and $y$

$$95^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2(x)(y)\cos 65^\circ$$

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.content:
The question asks for an equation relating $x$ and $y$. We present the equation directly as derived.

$$95^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2xy\cos 65^\circ$$

This equation is sufficient to earn the marks. You do not need to evaluate $95^2$ or $\cos 65^\circ$ unless asked to solve for a specific variable.

parts.content: Find the height $h$ of the drone above the square, correct to the nearest metre.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute $y = \dfrac{x\tan24^\circ}{\tan39^\circ}$ into the equation from part (c): <marks>M1</marks>
$$95^2 = x^2 + \left(\dfrac{x\tan24^\circ}{\tan39^\circ}\right)^2 - 2x\left(\dfrac{x\tan24^\circ}{\tan39^\circ}\right)\cos65^\circ$$
- Solve for $x$: $x \approx 103.8\text{ m}$ (and $y \approx 57.1\text{ m}$)
- Calculate height $h = x\tan24^\circ \approx 103.8\tan24^\circ \approx 46.2\text{ m}$
- State final answer rounded to nearest metre: $h \approx 46\text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define variables and height relations
steps.content:
Let $h$ be the height of the drone $PP_0$. Let $x = AP_0$ and $y = BP_0$ be the horizontal distances from the observation points.

From the **angles of elevation**, we can write $h$ in terms of $x$ and $y$:
- In $\triangle PAP_0$: $\tan 24^\circ = \frac{h}{x} \Rightarrow h = x\tan 24^\circ$
- In $\triangle PBP_0$: $\tan 39^\circ = \frac{h}{y} \Rightarrow h = y\tan 39^\circ$

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the drone is $24^\circ$ from $A$ and $39^\circ$ from $B$

steps.title: Express y in terms of x
steps.content:
Since both expressions equal $h$, we can equate them to express $y$ in terms of $x$:

$$x\tan 24^\circ = y\tan 39^\circ$$

$$y = x\frac{\tan 24^\circ}{\tan 39^\circ}$$

steps.title: Set up the Cosine Rule
steps.content:
Now consider the horizontal triangle $\triangle AP_0B$. We know side $c = AB = 95$, and the angle $C = \angle AP_0B = 65^\circ$. Using the **Cosine Rule** from the data booklet:

$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab\cos C$$

Substituting our values ($x$ for $AP_0$ and $y$ for $BP_0$):

$$95^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2xy\cos 65^\circ$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve for x
steps.content:
Substitute the expression for $y$ into the Cosine Rule equation:

$$95^2 = x^2 + \left(x\frac{\tan 24^\circ}{\tan 39^\circ}\right)^2 - 2x\left(x\frac{\tan 24^\circ}{\tan 39^\circ}\right)\cos 65^\circ$$

Factor out $x^2$ to solve:

$$95^2 = x^2 \left[ 1 + \left(\frac{\tan 24^\circ}{\tan 39^\circ}\right)^2 - 2\left(\frac{\tan 24^\circ}{\tan 39^\circ}\right)\cos 65^\circ \right]$$

Using a calculator to solve for $x$:

$$x \approx 103.804 \dots \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions:
Enter the equation in the Num Solv app:
95^2 = x^2 + (x*tan(24)/tan(39))^2 - 2*x*(x*tan(24)/tan(39))*cos(65)

ti84.instructions:
1. Calculate the ratio k: tan(24)/tan(39) -> Store as K
2. Calculate the bracket term: 1 + K^2 - 2*K*cos(65) -> Store as D
3. Calculate x: sqrt(95^2 / D)

desmos.instructions: Type the equation to find the intersection or solve directly.

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient of x² and solve

steps.title: Calculate the height
steps.content:
Now substitute $x$ back into the first height equation to find $h$:

$$h = x\tan 24^\circ$$

$$h \approx 103.804 \times \tan 24^\circ$$

$$h \approx 46.216 \dots \text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply the previous answer by tan(24)

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Round the result to the nearest metre as required:

$$h \approx 46 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Define variables and identify method
steps.content:
This question requires finding the height $h$ using information from both vertical and horizontal triangles. This falls under **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry**.

We will use the **Direct Height Substitution** method:
1. Express the horizontal distances $AP_0$ and $BP_0$ in terms of $h$.
2. Substitute these expressions into the Cosine Rule for the horizontal triangle $\triangle AP_0B$.
3. Solve for $h$.

steps.title: Express horizontal distances in terms of h
steps.content:
Let $h$ be the height of the drone $P$ above $P_0$. In the vertical right-angled triangles formed by the drone's elevation:

For point A:
$$\tan 24^\circ = \frac{h}{AP_0} \implies AP_0 = \frac{h}{\tan 24^\circ}$$

For point B:
$$\tan 39^\circ = \frac{h}{BP_0} \implies BP_0 = \frac{h}{\tan 39^\circ}$$

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the drone is $24^\circ$ from $A$ and $39^\circ$ from $B$

steps.title: Apply Cosine Rule to horizontal triangle
steps.content:
Now we consider the horizontal triangle $\triangle AP_0B$. We know the side lengths $AP_0$ and $BP_0$ (in terms of $h$), the angle between them ($65^\circ$), and the opposite side ($95$ m).

Using the **Cosine Rule** from the data booklet:

$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$$

Substituting our values:

$$95^2 = (AP_0)^2 + (BP_0)^2 - 2(AP_0)(BP_0) \cos 65^\circ$$

steps.title: Substitute and factor out h²
steps.content:
Substitute the expressions for $AP_0$ and $BP_0$ into the equation and factor out $h^2$:

$$\begin{aligned} 95^2 &= \left(\frac{h}{\tan 24^\circ}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{h}{\tan 39^\circ}\right)^2 - 2\left(\frac{h}{\tan 24^\circ}\right)\left(\frac{h}{\tan 39^\circ}\right)\cos 65^\circ \\ 95^2 &= h^2 \left[ \frac{1}{(\tan 24^\circ)^2} + \frac{1}{(\tan 39^\circ)^2} - \frac{2\cos 65^\circ}{\tan 24^\circ \tan 39^\circ} \right] \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for h using calculator
steps.content:
Calculate the value of the term in brackets, then divide $95^2$ by this result and take the square root.

$$h = \sqrt{\frac{95^2}{\frac{1}{(\tan 24^\circ)^2} + \frac{1}{(\tan 39^\circ)^2} - \frac{2\cos 65^\circ}{\tan 24^\circ \tan 39^\circ}}}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Calculate the bracket term:
(1/tan(24)^2) + (1/tan(39)^2) - (2*cos(65))/(tan(24)*tan(39)) [EXE]

2. Solve for h:
sqrt(95^2 / Ans) [EXE]

ti84.instructions:
1. Calculate the bracket term:
(1/tan(24)^2) + (1/tan(39)^2) - (2*cos(65))/(tan(24)*tan(39)) [ENTER]
Result: 4.225...

2. Solve for h:
sqrt(95^2 / Ans) [ENTER]

desmos.instructions:
Type the expression directly:
sqrt( 95^2 / ( (1/tan(24 degrees))^2 + (1/tan(39 degrees))^2 - (2*cos(65 degrees))/(tan(24 degrees)*tan(39 degrees)) ) )

calculator.description: Calculate the denominator term first, then solve for h

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives:

$$h \approx 46.216...$$

Rounding to the nearest metre:

$$h \approx 46 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Define variables and triangles
steps.content:
Let $h$ be the height of the drone. Let $x$ be the horizontal distance $AP_0$ and $y$ be the horizontal distance $BP_0$.

From the question, we have a horizontal triangle $\triangle AP_0B$ with:
- Side $AB = 95$ m
- $\angle AP_0B = 65^\circ$

We also have two vertical right-angled triangles from the angles of elevation:
- At $A$, angle of elevation is $24^\circ$
- At $B$, angle of elevation is $39^\circ$

highlights.text: distance between A and B is 95 m

steps.title: Express horizontal distances
steps.content:
Using the tangent ratio in the vertical triangles, we can express the horizontal distances $x$ and $y$ in terms of height $h$:

$$\tan 24^\circ = \frac{h}{x} \implies x = \frac{h}{\tan 24^\circ}$$

$$\tan 39^\circ = \frac{h}{y} \implies y = \frac{h}{\tan 39^\circ}$$

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is Degree

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the drone is 24^\circ from A and 39^\circ from B

steps.title: Determine ratio of sides
steps.content:
Now we find the ratio between the side lengths $x$ and $y$:

$$\frac{x}{y} = \frac{\frac{h}{\tan 24^\circ}}{\frac{h}{\tan 39^\circ}} = \frac{\tan 39^\circ}{\tan 24^\circ}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate the ratio

steps.title: Apply Sine Rule
steps.content:
In the horizontal triangle $\triangle AP_0B$, let $\angle P_0AB = A$ and $\angle P_0BA = B$. The side opposite $\angle B$ is $x$ ($AP_0$) and the side opposite $\angle A$ is $y$ ($BP_0$).

Using the Sine Rule:
$$\frac{x}{\sin B} = \frac{y}{\sin A}$$

Rearranging to match our ratio:
$$\frac{x}{y} = \frac{\sin B}{\sin A} = \frac{\tan 39^\circ}{\tan 24^\circ} \approx 1.819$$

The sum of angles in $\triangle AP_0B$ is $180^\circ$, so:
$$A + B + 65^\circ = 180^\circ \implies B = 115^\circ - A$$

steps.title: Solve for base angles
steps.content:
Substitute $B = 115^\circ - A$ into the ratio equation:

$$\frac{\sin(115^\circ - A)}{\sin A} = 1.819$$

Using a graphing calculator, we can solve for $A$ by finding the intersection of $Y_1 = \frac{\sin(115^\circ - x)}{\sin x}$ and $Y_2 = 1.819$.

Solving gives:
$$A \approx 33.0^\circ$$
$$B = 115^\circ - 33.0^\circ = 82.0^\circ$$

gdc.instructions: Graph the functions f1(x) = sin(115-x)/sin(x) and f2(x) = 1.819 and find the intersection.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press Y=.
2. Enter Y1 = sin(115-X)/sin(X)
3. Enter Y2 = tan(39)/tan(24)
4. Press GRAPH (adjust Window to Xmin=0, Xmax=115)
5. Press 2nd > TRACE > 5:intersect

desmos.instructions: Graph y = sin(115-x)/sin(x) and y = 1.819. Find intersection.

calculator.description: Solve for angle A using graphing

steps.title: Calculate side length x
steps.content:
Now use the Sine Rule again to find side $x$ ($AP_0$) using the known side $AB = 95$ m:

$$\frac{x}{\sin B} = \frac{95}{\sin 65^\circ}$$

$$x = \frac{95 \cdot \sin 82.0^\circ}{\sin 65^\circ}$$

$$x \approx 103.8\text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate value

steps.title: Calculate height
steps.content:
Finally, calculate the height $h$ using the expression from Step 1:

$$h = x \tan 24^\circ$$

$$h \approx 103.8 \cdot \tan 24^\circ$$

$$h \approx 46.2\text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply previous answer by tan(24)

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to the nearest metre as required:

$$h \approx 46\text{ m}$$

parts.content:
The projection $P_0$ moves horizontally on a circular path of radius 95 m centered at $A$.  
Find the angle through which the bearing of $P_0$ from $A$ changes in radians, and the length of the arc traveled in metres when the bearing changes from $060^\circ$ to $180^\circ$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the change in bearing angle: $\theta = 180^\circ - 60^\circ = 120^\circ = \dfrac{2\pi}{3}\text{ rad}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the arc length: $s = 95 \times \dfrac{2\pi}{3} = \dfrac{190\pi}{3} \approx 199.0\text{ m}$ <marks>A1 A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Find the angle in degrees
steps.content:
First, we need to find the angle through which the drone's projection $P_0$ moves. The bearing changes from $060^\circ$ to $180^\circ$, so we calculate the difference between these two bearings:

$$\theta_{\text{deg}} = 180^\circ - 60^\circ = 120^\circ$$

steps.title: Convert angle to radians
steps.content:
The arc length formula uses radians, so we must convert our angle from degrees. We multiply by $\frac{\pi}{180}$:

$$\theta = 120 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{120\pi}{180} = \frac{2\pi}{3}\text{ rad}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate 120 times pi divided by 180

steps.title: State the arc length formula
steps.content:
To find the distance traveled along the circular path, we use the arc length formula found in the **Geometry & Trigonometry** section of the data booklet:

$$l = r\theta$$

where $l$ is the arc length, $r$ is the radius, and $\theta$ is the angle in radians.

steps.title: Calculate the arc length
steps.content:
Substitute the radius $r = 95\text{ m}$ and the angle $\theta = \frac{2\pi}{3}$ into the formula:

$$l = 95 \times \frac{2\pi}{3} = \frac{190\pi}{3}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply the radius by the angle in radians

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The exact arc length is $\frac{190\pi}{3}$ m. For the final answer, we can approximate this to 3 significant figures.

$$\frac{190\pi}{3} \approx 199\text{ m}$$

steps.title: Calculate the angle change
steps.content:
First, we find the change in the bearing angle in degrees by subtracting the initial bearing from the final bearing.

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta \theta &= 180^\circ - 60^\circ \\ &= 120^\circ \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Type 180 - 60

calculator.description: Calculate the difference between the two bearings

steps.title: Convert angle to radians
steps.content:
To answer the first part of the question, we convert the angle from degrees to radians by multiplying by $\frac{\pi}{180^\circ}$.

$$\theta_{\text{rad}} = 120 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{12\pi}{18} = \frac{2\pi}{3}\text{ rad}$$

steps.title: Find fraction of circle
steps.content:
Using the **Circumference Proportion** method, we first determine what fraction of a full circle this angle represents. A full circle is $360^\circ$.

$$\text{Fraction} = \frac{120^\circ}{360^\circ} = \frac{1}{3}$$

steps.title: Calculate full circumference
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the circumference of the full circular path using the radius $r = 95\text{ m}$.

$$C = 2\pi r = 2\pi(95) = 190\pi\text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Type 2 * pi * 95

steps.title: Calculate arc length
steps.content:
Finally, we find the arc length by multiplying the fraction of the circle by the total circumference.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Arc length} &= \text{Fraction} \times C \\ &= \frac{1}{3} \times 190\pi \\ &= \frac{190\pi}{3} \\ &\approx 199\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the result one would get using the formula $$l = \theta r$$ directly with radians.

steps.title: State the final answers
steps.content:
The required answers are:
- Angle change: $$\frac{2\pi}{3}\text{ rad}$$
- Arc length: $$199\text{ m}$$ (to 3 significant figures)

parts.content: If $T_A = k|AP|$ and $T_B = k|BP|$, find $\dfrac{T_A}{T_B}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate distances $AP$ and $BP$:
$AP = \sqrt{x^2 + h^2} \approx \sqrt{103.8^2 + 46.2^2} \approx 113.6\text{ m}$ <marks>M1</marks>
$BP = \sqrt{57.1^2 + 46.2^2} \approx 73.4\text{ m}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate ratio: $\dfrac{T_A}{T_B} \approx \dfrac{113.6}{73.4} \approx 1.55$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Simplify the ratio expression
steps.content:
The question asks for the ratio $\frac{T_A}{T_B}$. We are given expressions for $T_A$ and $T_B$ in terms of the cable lengths and a constant $k$. We can simplify the ratio immediately:

$$\frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{k|AP|}{k|BP|} = \frac{|AP|}{|BP|}$$

Since $k$ cancels out, we just need to find the ratio of the lengths of the two cables, $|AP|$ and $|BP|$.

steps.title: Recall values from previous parts
steps.content:
From the work done in previous parts of this question, we have the height of the drone and the horizontal distances from the points $P_0$ to $A$ and $B$.

Height $h \approx 46.2\text{ m}$
Horizontal distance $AP_0 \approx 103.8\text{ m}$
Horizontal distance $BP_0 \approx 57.1\text{ m}$

steps.title: Calculate length of cable AP
steps.content:
To find the length of the cable $|AP|$, we view the triangle $APP_0$ as a right-angled triangle with height $h$ and base $AP_0$. Using Pythagoras' theorem:

$$|AP| = \sqrt{(AP_0)^2 + h^2}$$

$$|AP| = \sqrt{103.8^2 + 46.2^2}$$

$$|AP| \approx 113.6\text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the square root calculation:

steps.title: Calculate length of cable BP
steps.content:
Similarly, for cable $|BP|$, we use the right-angled triangle $BPP_0$ with height $h$ and base $BP_0$. Using Pythagoras' theorem:

$$|BP| = \sqrt{(BP_0)^2 + h^2}$$

$$|BP| = \sqrt{57.1^2 + 46.2^2}$$

$$|BP| \approx 73.4\text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the square root calculation:

steps.title: Calculate the final ratio
steps.content:
Now we divide the calculated lengths to find the ratio $\frac{T_A}{T_B}$:

$$\frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{113.6}{73.4}$$

$$\frac{T_A}{T_B} \approx 1.55$$

ti84.instructions: Divide the results:

calculator.description: Divide the two lengths to find the ratio

steps.title: Simplify the ratio expression
steps.content:
The question asks for the ratio $\frac{T_A}{T_B}$ given the expressions for the tensions. We can substitute the given formulas and simplify by cancelling the constant $k$.

$$\frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{k|AP|}{k|BP|} = \frac{AP}{BP}$$

steps.title: Express AP using sine
steps.content:
Let $h$ be the vertical height of the drone $P$ above the ground point $P_0$. In the right-angled vertical triangle formed by points $A$, $P_0$, and $P$, the angle of elevation is $24^\circ$.

Using the definition of sine:
$$\sin \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$$

$$\sin(24^\circ) = \frac{h}{AP} \implies AP = \frac{h}{\sin(24^\circ)}$$

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the drone is $24^\circ$ from $A$

steps.title: Express BP using sine
steps.content:
Similarly, in the vertical triangle formed by points $B$, $P_0$, and $P$, the angle of elevation is $39^\circ$.

Using the definition of sine again:

$$\sin(39^\circ) = \frac{h}{BP} \implies BP = \frac{h}{\sin(39^\circ)}$$

steps.title: Substitute and cancel height
steps.content:
Now substitute these expressions into our ratio for $\frac{AP}{BP}$. The common height $h$ appears in both the numerator and denominator, so it cancels out.

$$\frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{AP}{BP} = \frac{\frac{h}{\sin(24^\circ)}}{\frac{h}{\sin(39^\circ)}} = \frac{\sin(39^\circ)}{\sin(24^\circ)}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final ratio
steps.content:
Evaluate the trigonometric ratio using a calculator.

$$\frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{\sin(39^\circ)}{\sin(24^\circ)} \approx 1.547$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the quotient of the sine values.

ti84.instructions: Ensure you are in Degree mode. Calculate sin(39)/sin(24).

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to 3 significant figures:

$$\frac{T_A}{T_B} \approx 1.55$$

questions.specification:
A cargo container $P$ is lifted by two cranes anchored at points $A$ and $B$ on a dock.
The bases $A$ and $B$ are 60 m apart. The container is directly above point $P_0$, and $\angle AP_0B = 50^\circ$ in the horizontal triangle $ABP_0$.

At a given instant, the angle of elevation of the container is $22^\circ$ from $A$ and $35^\circ$ from $B$.
